title: More Advanced Accounting
description: Deferred taxes, stock-based compensation, inventory methods, lease accounting, and other topics that come up in more technical IB interviews.

sections.title: Deferred Taxes (DTA and DTL)
sections.body:
Deferred taxes arise because accounting rules (GAAP) and tax rules sometimes recognize revenue and expenses at different times.

Deferred Tax Liability (DTL): You owe more tax in the future than you've paid so far.
• Example: Accelerated depreciation for taxes. A company depreciates an asset faster on its tax return than on its income statement. This means it pays less tax now, but will owe more later. So a liability builds up.
• On the balance sheet: a non-current liability.
• If DTL increases → the company paid less tax than its income statement shows → source of cash in CFO.

Deferred Tax Asset (DTA): You've paid more tax than you currently owe (or will owe less in the future).
• Example: Net operating losses (NOLs) carried forward. A company had a loss last year and can use it to reduce future taxable income.
• On the balance sheet: a non-current asset.
• If DTA increases → source of cash in CFO (tax you've effectively prepaid or will save).

Interview tip: Most common answer format is 'Is the DTA/DTL increasing or decreasing, and what does that mean for CFO?'
keyPoints: DTL: paid less tax than income suggests — liability, owes more in future
DTA: paid more tax than income suggests — asset, will save in future
Increase in DTL = source of cash in CFO (like AP going up)
Increase in DTA = use of cash in CFO (like AR going up)
Common source: accelerated tax depreciation (DTL) and NOL carry-forwards (DTA)

sections.title: Stock-Based Compensation (SBC)
sections.body:
Stock-based compensation is when a company pays employees with stock or options instead of (or in addition to) cash.

On the Income Statement: SBC is an expense that reduces operating income and net income, just like a salary. It appears in OpEx or COGS depending on who receives it.

On the Cash Flow Statement: SBC is a non-cash expense (no cash actually leaves the company for the grant itself). It gets added back in CFO, similar to D&A.

On the Balance Sheet: When shares are issued for SBC, equity increases (additional paid-in capital goes up). Retained earnings fall from the expense, but APIC rises from the issuance — net effect depends on timing and structure.

Why it matters: SBC is large at tech companies. Including it vs. excluding it significantly changes apparent profitability. Be careful — many companies report 'adjusted EBITDA' or 'adjusted net income' that excludes SBC. Analysts and investors debate whether SBC should be treated as a real cost (it is economically dilutive to shareholders).

Common interview answer: 'SBC is non-cash, so we add it back in CFO. It reduces net income and EBIT, but doesn't reduce operating cash flow directly.'
keyPoints: SBC is an operating expense — reduces EBIT and net income
SBC is non-cash — added back in CFO (like D&A)
Equity increases when shares are issued (APIC up); retained earnings fall from expense
SBC dilutes existing shareholders — real economic cost even if non-cash
Watch out for adjusted metrics that exclude SBC — know the difference

sections.title: Inventory Accounting: FIFO, LIFO, and Weighted Average
sections.body:
When a company sells goods, it needs a method to determine which inventory cost to recognize in COGS. The physical goods may be identical, but the accounting choice affects reported profit.

FIFO (First In, First Out): Assume oldest inventory is sold first.
• In a rising-price environment: COGS is lower (older, cheaper items sold first), so gross profit and net income are higher. Inventory on the balance sheet reflects recent, higher costs.

LIFO (Last In, First Out): Assume newest inventory is sold first.
• In a rising-price environment: COGS is higher (newer, more expensive items sold first), so gross profit and net income are lower. Lower taxes.
• Note: LIFO is allowed under US GAAP but banned under IFRS. If you're comparing a US company using LIFO to a non-US company, you need to adjust.

Weighted Average: Use the average cost of all inventory.
• Smooths out price fluctuations; results between FIFO and LIFO.

The LIFO Reserve: Companies using LIFO must disclose the LIFO reserve — the difference between LIFO and FIFO inventory values. To compare companies, you can add the LIFO reserve to inventory to convert to FIFO.
keyPoints: FIFO (rising prices): lower COGS, higher income, higher inventory on BS
LIFO (rising prices): higher COGS, lower income, lower taxes, lower inventory on BS
LIFO allowed under GAAP only — not IFRS
LIFO reserve: difference between LIFO and FIFO inventory — add it back to convert
Inventory choice doesn't affect cash — just the timing of when cost hits the P&L

sections.title: Lease Accounting (ASC 842 / IFRS 16)
sections.body:
Under the old rules, companies could keep operating leases entirely off the balance sheet. Under current rules (ASC 842 in the US, IFRS 16 internationally), most leases must now be capitalized.

Operating Lease (still the most common type):
• Balance sheet: a Right-of-Use (ROU) asset and a lease liability appear.
• Income statement: lease expense is one line item in operating expenses (like old operating lease treatment).
• Cash flow: the payment is in CFO.

Finance Lease (capital lease):
• Balance sheet: same — ROU asset and lease liability.
• Income statement: split into depreciation (in EBITDA calculation) and interest expense (below EBIT).
• Cash flow: principal is in CFF, interest in CFO or CFF depending on policy.

Key interview point: EBITDA is affected differently. For an operating lease, the lease expense is above EBITDA. For a finance lease, only the depreciation piece is above EBITDA; interest is below. This is why comparing EBITDA across companies can require adjustments for lease classification.

Old vs. new: Prior to ASC 842, operating leases were fully off-balance-sheet. Now they show up — this inflates assets and liabilities but doesn't change underlying economics.
keyPoints: ASC 842/IFRS 16: most leases now on balance sheet as ROU asset + lease liability
Operating lease: single lease expense in OpEx; payment in CFO
Finance lease: depreciation in OpEx + interest below EBIT; principal in CFF
Operating lease expense is above EBITDA; finance lease interest is below
New rules changed optics of leverage ratios — same economics, different appearance

sections.title: Goodwill, Intangibles, and Purchase Price Allocation
sections.body:
When one company acquires another at a premium to book value, the acquirer must allocate the purchase price to the target's identifiable assets and liabilities at fair value. Anything left over is goodwill.

Purchase Price Allocation (PPA):
1. Identify and value target's assets at fair market value (often higher than book)
2. Identify and value target's liabilities
3. Net fair value of assets/liabilities = adjusted net assets
4. Purchase price − adjusted net assets = goodwill

Goodwill on the Balance Sheet:
• Stays there until impaired (no amortization under GAAP)
• Under IFRS, some intangibles with finite lives are amortized
• If the business underperforms expectations, goodwill gets written down (impaired): non-cash charge on IS, reduces goodwill on BS

Identified Intangibles (not goodwill):
• Customer relationships, patents, trademarks, technology
• These have finite lives and are amortized — shows up as D&A, which hits EBIT
• Added back in CFO (non-cash)

Goodwill impairment:
• Non-cash charge reduces IS income and BS goodwill
• No tax effect (goodwill not deductible in most cases)
• No cash flow impact
keyPoints: Goodwill = purchase price minus fair value of net assets acquired
Not amortized under GAAP — stays on BS until impaired
Impairment: non-cash charge on IS, reduces BS goodwill, no CFS impact
Identified intangibles (patents, customer lists) are amortized — non-cash, add back in CFO
PPA step-up in asset values creates D&A that reduces earnings post-acquisition

sections.title: Depreciation Methods and PP&E
sections.body:
Depreciation is the systematic allocation of the cost of a long-lived asset over its useful life. It's a non-cash expense — reduces income but not cash.

Straight-Line Depreciation:
• Annual depreciation = (Cost − Salvage Value) / Useful Life
• Example: $100 asset, $0 salvage, 10-year life → $10/year
• Most common for GAAP reporting

Accelerated Depreciation (e.g. Double Declining Balance):
• Higher depreciation in early years, lower later
• Used for tax purposes (lowers taxes now, creates DTL)
• DDB: 2 × (1/useful life) × book value each year

PP&E Roll-Forward:
Beginning PP&E + CapEx − Depreciation − Asset Sales = Ending PP&E

Amortization vs. Depreciation:
• Depreciation: tangible assets (equipment, buildings)
• Amortization: intangible assets (patents, customer lists, capitalized software)
• Both are non-cash; both added back in CFO
• Together they form D&A on the income statement

Interview tip: Know the PP&E roll-forward cold. If CapEx = $50 and depreciation = $30, PP&E net increases by $20. That $50 is in CFI as an outflow; the $30 is a non-cash add-back in CFO.
keyPoints: Straight-line: equal charge each year; most common for GAAP
Accelerated: more depreciation early — reduces taxes now, creates DTL
PP&E roll-forward: beginning + CapEx − D&A − disposals = ending
Amortization: same concept for intangible assets
Both are non-cash — added back in CFO, reduce income on IS

sections.title: Shareholders' Equity and Key Ratios
sections.body:
Shareholders' equity is the residual interest in assets after liabilities. It changes each period:

Beginning Equity
+ Net Income
− Dividends
+ Stock Issuances (APIC)
− Share Buybacks (Treasury Stock)
+ SBC (non-cash, increases APIC)
= Ending Equity

Key Financial Ratios:

Liquidity:
• Current Ratio = Current Assets / Current Liabilities (>1 = can pay short-term obligations)
• Quick Ratio = (Current Assets − Inventory) / Current Liabilities (stricter)

Profitability:
• Gross Margin = Gross Profit / Revenue
• EBITDA Margin = EBITDA / Revenue
• Net Margin = Net Income / Revenue
• Return on Equity (ROE) = Net Income / Equity
• Return on Assets (ROA) = Net Income / Assets

Leverage:
• Debt / EBITDA — key credit metric, how many years to pay off debt
• Net Debt / EBITDA — same but subtracts cash from debt
• Interest Coverage = EBITDA / Interest Expense

Why this matters: These ratios are used in credit analysis, M&A screening, and comp analysis. Interviewers may ask you to interpret them or compare two companies.
keyPoints: Equity roll-forward: beginning + NI − dividends + issuances − buybacks = ending
Current ratio and quick ratio measure short-term liquidity
EBITDA margin and net margin measure profitability at different stages
Debt/EBITDA and interest coverage measure leverage and debt serviceability
ROE = NI/Equity; ROA = NI/Assets — return metrics
